title: Self-Talk — The Soundtrack
description: System 1 self-talk is reactive. System 2 self-talk is planned. You can choose which runs.

blocks.content: Three hours into a race. Legs filing a formal complaint. Pace slipping. Then the internal monologue cranks the volume: 'This is going to be a long day.' 'Everyone's passing me.' 'I'm not built for this distance.' That's your self-talk. And right now, it's working against you.

You can't stop the voice — thoughts don't have an off switch. But you CAN answer them. You can curate the soundtrack. Trained, deliberate self-talk measurably improves endurance performance. Not by a little. By a lot.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a scripted Race Soundtrack with 6 phrases assigned to specific race moments.

blocks.question: Retrieval from Lesson 3.3: An effective If-Then plan must be:
blocks.explanation: Correct. 'If [specific trigger], then I will [specific action].' Specificity is what converts deliberation into automation.

options.text: Specific in both trigger and response
Flexible enough to adapt to whatever happens in the moment

blocks.content: System 1 self-talk is reactive — the chimp talking. System 2 self-talk is deliberate — the coach responding. You can't control what System 1 says. But you can train System 2 to answer.

tabs.label: Instructional Self-Talk
tabs.content: Technique-focused cues that direct your body: 'Light feet.' 'Shoulders down.' 'Breathe low.' Short, operational commands — not motivational, but mechanical. Works best for fine motor skills and technical execution. When you need precision, use instructional cues.

tabs.label: Motivational Self-Talk
tabs.content: Psyche-up phrases about arousal, confidence, and drive: 'You've done this before.' 'Stay in it.' 'You're tougher than this moment.' These regulate how you feel, not what you do. Works best for endurance tasks and arousal regulation — managing the suffering of the last hour.

items.title: Hatzigeorgiadis et al. — 32-Study Meta-Analysis
items.content: Self-talk produced significant performance benefits across every study. Instructional self-talk was most effective for technique tasks. Motivational self-talk was most effective for endurance and strength. Critically: trained, rehearsed self-talk was more effective than spontaneous. You can't wing it. The phrases need to be chosen, practiced, and automated before race day.

items.title: Third-Person Self-Talk (Kross et al., 2014)
items.content: Using your own name creates psychological distance. 'Matt, settle in' processes differently than 'I need to settle in.' The first feels like coaching. The second feels like desperation. Linguistic distance creates the verbal equivalent of the Gap Check — space between stimulus and response, but with language instead of breath.

items.title: Cross-Reference: Performance Statements (Lesson 2.4)
items.content: Performance Statements are identity-level self-talk — who you are as an athlete. The Race Soundtrack adds real-time tactical self-talk — what to say at specific moments during execution. Both are System 2 tools. Performance Statements anchor identity. The Soundtrack directs in-race behavior.

blocks.content: This builds on Performance Statements (Lesson 2.4). There you built identity-level phrases. Here you assign tactical self-talk to specific race moments.

items.label: Write 3 Instructional Cues
items.detail: Two to four words max. They tell your body what to do. Examples: 'Light feet, quick turnover.' 'Shoulders down, hands loose.' 'Breathe low, stay tall.' Your three — specific to the technical breakdowns you have when tired.

items.label: Write 3 Motivational Phrases
items.detail: Emotionally loaded, personally meaningful. Examples: 'You've been here before and you survived.' 'This is what the training was for.' Use third-person if it resonates: '[Your name], hold this pace.'

items.label: Assign each phrase to a race moment
items.detail: This separates a script from a wish. Example: First 30 min → 'Patient. Smooth. Save it.' Technical sections → 'Eyes ahead, weight centered.' Mid-race fatigue → 'This is the work. Stay in it.' When passed → 'Run your race. Your pace.' Final hour → 'Quick feet, tall spine.' Want to quit → 'You don't quit.'

items.label: Rehearse in training
items.detail: Say them out loud during hard intervals. Deploy during boring easy sessions. The words need to be automated — instantly available without searching. If you have to think about what to say when you're suffering, it's too late.

blocks.title: Race Soundtrack Card (6 slots with assigned moments)

blocks.content: Your commitment: I will rehearse my Race Soundtrack during 2 training sessions this week.
You're going to talk to yourself during the race anyway. Decide what you're going to say.
Next — watching your thoughts without getting caught in them. Mindfulness isn't what you think it is.
